The raw API is for cases where the size/alignment is only known at runtime, which is not your situation.

- `create` / `destroy`: <https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/std/#std.mem.Allocator.create>

**4. `@ptrCast` from `[*]u8` to `*align(N) State` without `@alignCast`**

`rawAlloc` returns `[*]u8` (alignment 1). Going to a higher-aligned pointer is an alignment change and conventionally requires `@alignCast`. - <https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/#ptrCast>

**7. `buffer_modification_index` reset semantics**
The else-branch unconditionally writes `buffer_modification_index = -1` on every literal append. The doc-comment says it marks the *earliest* modified position. Setting it to `-1` is fine because appending a brand-new span is not "modifying an existing span," but if a previous Telex pass set it and is followed by a literal char, that prior modification info is lost the same tick. No finding on the core `init` shape itself. Your rationale is sound: zeroing would create invalid `Span` values, so “uninitialized until written, never read unless logically valid” is the correct invariant.

`pub fn sizeof() usize` in [`State.sizeof`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L212-L215) is also fine. At the FFI boundary, `usize` mapping to C `size_t` is normal. ## What To Expose

You have three practical choices.

1. Treat alignment as part of the public ABI and export it explicitly.
2. Expose a public opaque FFI type whose layout already carries the alignment requirement.
3. Generate a C header that lets Swift/C read both size and alignment from the type itself.

For your current design, option 1 is the smallest and clearest: the library should publish both `size` and `align`.

## What Swift Must Do

Swift must allocate the state buffer with:

- capacity at least `State` size
- alignment at least `State` alignment

If Swift is allocating raw memory, the relevant contract is the alignment parameter of its raw allocation API, not just the byte count. If Swift wraps the storage in a typed container, then the container’s alignment must be at least the exported alignment.

## TigerBeetle Alignment Style

TigerBeetle’s pattern is stricter than “just return size”:

- size is pinned with compile-time assertions
- alignment is pinned with compile-time assertions
- opaque FFI handles are shaped so alignment is an intentional ABI property

That is the important alignment lesson from TigerBeetle: do not leave alignment implicit.

## What To Document

Your public FFI contract should say:

- `lex_state_sizeof()` returns the required storage size
- caller must also satisfy the required alignment for the state object
- the pointer passed to init and all other APIs must be aligned for `State`
- size and alignment are ABI, so changing either is a breaking FFI change

- `@sizeOf`: <https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/>
- `extern struct`: <https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/>
- alignment and pointer rules in the language reference: <https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/>
- `export` and C ABI behavior: <https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/>

TigerBeetle references worth studying again:

- opaque FFI handle with size/alignment assertions: <https://github.com/tigerbeetle/tigerbeetle/blob/main/src/clients/c/tb_client_exports.zig>

In `lex_add` (line 237), you make a **copy** of the state instead of working with the pointer:

```zig

```

`State` is a value type (`extern struct`), so `state_pointer.*` dereferences and copies the entire struct into a new local `state`. Mục đích của chương trình là để hiểu rõ hơn về tính khả thi của cấu trúc chương trình hỗ trợ đa nền

trình của

## Findings

- Medium: The switch from [`extern struct` to `struct`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L194-L211) is only idiomatic if `State` is now intended to be opaque internal state, not a field-layout ABI type. Using `?u8` for [`buffer_modification_index` and `literal_index`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L201-L209) is good Zig for internal state, but it pushes the representation toward “Zig-owned layout”. That is a good trade if foreign callers only allocate raw storage using `lex_state_size` and `lex_state_alignment` and never inspect fields. It is not the right trade if you still want `State` itself to be a C-layout-visible struct. In other words: this is idiomatic Zig, but only under the “opaque FFI handle” interpretation.

- Low: Several comments still describe the old sentinel model after the type has moved to optionals, which now fights the new type design. The stale pieces are in the [`literal_index` comment](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L204-L209), the plain-append branch comment for [`buffer_modification_index`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L276-L281), and the test comments that still say “expect -1” in the assertions around [`buffer_modification_index` and `literal_index`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L398-L435). One of the main benefits of `?u8` is that “unset” stops being a fake numeric value. If the comments still talk as if `-1` exists, readers lose that benefit.

- Low: The optional handling style is slightly inconsistent. The invariant for [`buffer_modification_index`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L224-L225) uses `== null or ... .?`, while [`literal_index`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L227-L233) uses payload capture. In Zig, once a value is optional, the most idiomatic style is usually `if (opt) |value| { ... }` or `orelse`, because it unwraps once and avoids a separate force-unwrap. Your `literal_index` block is the more Zig-native style; the `buffer_modification_index` assert is correct, just a bit less idiomatic.

## Overall Assessment

The core change itself is a good Zig move. Replacing sentinel `-1` with [`?u8`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L201-L209) is more idiomatic than keeping a signed integer just to encode absence. It improves the domain model in three ways:

- The type now says “index or unset” directly.
- The payload is unsigned, which matches an index better than `i8`.
- You removed unnecessary casts like the old `@intCast(...)` in places such as the assignments at [`L288`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L286-L288), [`L295`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L293-L295), and [`L304`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L303-L304), because the arithmetic and field types now line up naturally.

The best part of the change is the payload-capture use for [`literal_index`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L227-L233). That is exactly how Zig wants optionals to be handled: check presence and bind the non-null value in one step. That matches the Zig language reference and also matches TigerBeetle-style code, which regularly uses `?u8`/`?u32` plus `null` checks and payload capture instead of sentinel integers.

The tests also moved in the right direction. Assertions like [`expectEqual(null, state.buffer_modification_index)`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L400-L404) express the state more clearly than “expect `-1`”. The remaining integer expectations such as [`expectEqual(16, state.literal_index)`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L431-L435) and [`expectEqual(0, state.buffer_modification_index)`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L479-L481) are still valid Zig because payload values coerce to the optional type, so that part is fine.

## What I Would Change Yourself

If you want to polish this by hand without changing the design:

1. Decide explicitly whether [`State`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L194-L211) is an opaque Zig-owned state object or a C-layout struct. If opaque, keeping plain `struct` plus optionals is the more idiomatic Zig choice.
2. Update every stale `-1` comment so the prose matches the new type-level model: `null` means unset, non-null means a valid index.
3. Make the optional style more uniform by favoring payload capture for both optionals, not only for [`literal_index`](file:///<redacted>/lex/src/liblex.zig#L227-L233).

## References

Official Zig docs worth rereading for this change:

- Optionals: `https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/#Optionals`
- `if` with optional payload capture: `https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/#if`
- Regular `struct`: `https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/#struct`
- `extern struct`: `https://ziglang.org/documentation/master/#extern-struct`

TigerBeetle-style reference point:

- TigerBeetle commonly uses `?u8` / `?u32 = null` for lifecycle or cursor state and unwraps with assertions or payload capture, rather than keeping sentinel integers in scalar fields.

I did not change any code. The optional migration itself is mostly idiomatic Zig; the main question is whether the `State` layout is meant to remain part of your FFI contract or not.
